1.synthesis: Paul uses the analogy of marriage to explain the believer's relationship to the law: a woman is bound to her husband while he lives, but free when he dies (Romans 7:1–3). Likewise, believers have died to the law through the body of Christ so that they may belong to another—to him who was raised from the dead—in order to bear fruit for God (Romans 7:4). When they were in the flesh, sinful passions aroused by the law were at work in their members to bear fruit for death; but now they are released from the law, having died to what held them captive, so that they serve not under the old written code but in the new life of the Spirit (Romans 7:5–6).
1.historical_context: The marriage analogy is chosen because it addresses a Jewish audience familiar with Torah (v.1: 'I am speaking to those who know the law'). The analogy is legally precise: death dissolves the marriage bond, freeing both parties. Paul's argument is that the believer's death with Christ (Romans 6:3–4) constitutes the death that terminates the law-bond. The phrase 'sinful passions aroused by the law' (v.5) anticipates the fuller argument of 7:7–25: the law, though holy, becomes the occasion through which sin manifests its power. The 'new life of the Spirit' (v.6) transitions to the full pneumatology of chapter 8.
1.literary_structure: This brief passage functions as a transitional bridge between the death-to-sin argument of chapter 6 and the law-and-sin analysis of 7:7–25. The marriage analogy (vv.1–3) is an illustration only; the application is in verse 4 and developed in verses 5–6. The contrast between 'old written code' and 'new life of the Spirit' (v.6) establishes the pneumatological horizon that chapter 8 will fill in. The section answers the implicit objection raised by 6:14 ('you are not under law'): what does that mean for those who have always understood themselves to be defined by Torah?
1.theological_application: The marriage analogy establishes a crucial principle: the believer's relationship to the law has changed, not because the law changed but because the believer died with Christ and now belongs to another Lord. The law's authority is not revoked but rendered inapplicable to those who have moved, through death and resurrection, outside its jurisdiction. The new life is not lawless but Spirit-directed—the same righteous requirements of the law are now written on the heart by the Spirit who indwells believers. The transition from 'old written code' to 'new life of the Spirit' is not an abandonment of moral order but its internalization and empowerment: what the law demanded but could not produce, the Spirit now enables. The church must resist both antinomianism (the law doesn't matter now) and legalism (the law-code governs spiritual life) in favor of the third option Paul is moving toward: Spirit-directed life that fulfills the law's righteous intent from the inside out. This is the new-covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 being realized through union with the risen Christ.
1.christology: 'Belonging to him who was raised from the dead' (v.4) is the christological center of the passage: the believer's new identity is defined by belonging to the risen Christ, not by being under Torah. The death that terminates the law-bond is the believer's death with Christ; the new life is the believer's life in union with the risen one. To bear fruit for God is to live the resurrection life that Christ himself lives—the same life principle that operates in the risen Christ now operates in those who belong to him. The shift from 'fruit for death' (v.5) to 'fruit for God' (v.4) is the eschatological reversal that the resurrection of Christ makes possible: the power of death over human existence is broken by the one who has passed through death and emerged as the source of new life for all who are united to him by faith.

7.synthesis: Paul addresses the objection: is the law sin? By no means—yet he would not have known sin except through the law; he would not have known covetousness if the law had not said 'You shall not covet' (Romans 7:7). Sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, produced in him all covetousness; apart from the law, sin lies dead (Romans 7:8). He was alive once apart from the law, but when the commandment came, sin came alive and he died; the commandment that promised life proved to be death (Romans 7:9–10). Sin deceived him and through the commandment killed him (Romans 7:11). The law is holy, and the commandment holy and righteous and good; yet what is good became death to him—so that sin through what is good would produce death, so that sin through the commandment would become sinful beyond measure (Romans 7:12–13).
7.historical_context: The autobiographical 'I' of this chapter is one of the most debated interpretive questions in Pauline studies: does it describe Paul before conversion, Paul as a representative Jew, or the regenerate believer's ongoing struggle? In 7:7–13, the narrative of sin's activation through commandment echoes the Eden narrative (Genesis 3:1–6): the serpent used the commandment ('you shall not eat') to produce the very transgression the command was designed to prevent. The commandment 'You shall not covet' (Exodus 20:17) is the tenth commandment and unique among the Decalogue in prohibiting an internal desire rather than an external act—making it the commandment most suited to Paul's analysis of sin's interior operation.
7.literary_structure: The passage moves through a narrative arc: the commandment arrives → sin seizes the opportunity → covetousness multiplies → the person dies. The verb sequence ('I was alive,' 'sin came alive,' 'I died') traces the tragic reversal. The doxological conclusion—'the law is holy, and the commandment holy and righteous and good' (v.12)—clears the law of any guilt; the culprit is sin, not the law. Verse 13 then heightens the paradox: through what is good (the commandment), sin becomes even more sinful—the law exposes sin's true character as the willful perversion of every good gift. This sets up 7:14–25, where the conflict is internalized even further.
7.theological_application: The law-and-sin analysis of Romans 7:7–13 is the definitive New Testament account of why moral instruction alone cannot produce moral transformation. The commandment that names the wrong thing does not prevent it—it can activate the very desire it prohibits, because sin is not primarily ignorance but rebellion. This is the structural insight that distinguishes Paul's soteriology from every moralistic religion: the problem is not that people don't know what is right but that they cannot do it by willpower, education, or law. The law's holiness is not in question; its limitation is that it can diagnose sin but not cure it. The church's preaching must take this seriously: we cannot produce transformed lives by commanding moral behavior more insistently; we need the Spirit's power to accomplish what the law cannot. The commandment reveals sin as sin; only grace addresses sin as a power. The law remains the standard of God's righteous requirement; the Spirit is the power that fulfills that standard from within.
7.christology: The commandment's activation of sin and production of death mirrors what happened in Eden—and what Christ reverses. Adam's transgression through the commandment brought death to all; Christ's obedience through the law ('born under the law,' Galatians 4:4) brings life to all who are united to him. The analysis of sin's power in 7:7–13 sets the stage for 8:1–4: what the law could not do because it was weakened by the flesh, God did by sending his own Son—condemning sin in the flesh so that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in those who walk by the Spirit. Christ is the one in whom the law's full demand is met without sin seizing the opportunity; and believers in him share that fulfillment through Spirit-union rather than command-keeping.

14.synthesis: Paul says the law is spiritual, but he is fleshly, sold under sin. He does not understand his own actions: he does not do what he wants, but does the very thing he hates (Romans 7:14–15). If he does what he does not want, he agrees that the law is good; but it is no longer he who does it but sin that dwells within him (Romans 7:16–17). Nothing good dwells in him—in his flesh; for he has the desire to do what is right, but not the ability (Romans 7:18). He finds a law: when he wants to do right, evil lies close at hand. He delights in God's law in his inner being, but sees another law waging war against the law of his mind, making him captive to sin (Romans 7:21–23). 'Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?' Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, he himself serves the law of God with his mind, but with his flesh the law of sin (Romans 7:24–25).
14.historical_context: The shift to present tense ('I do,' 'I want,' 'I find') in 7:14–25 after the past tense of 7:7–13 has generated two major interpretive camps: (1) Paul describes his pre-conversion experience as a Jew under law; (2) Paul describes the experience of the regenerate believer in ongoing conflict with indwelling sin. The pastoral tradition (Augustine after 397, Luther, Calvin, Reformed reading) strongly favors (2), noting that the 'inner delight in God's law' (v.22) is characteristically regenerate, and that the resolution in 8:1–4 follows immediately. The cry 'Who will deliver me?' (v.24) is the existential cry of someone who knows the good but experiences profound powerlessness—a regenerate, not merely pre-converted, experience. 'Body of death' may echo the ancient punishment of chaining a murderer to his victim's corpse until the decomposition killed him—a vivid image of being bound to mortality and sin.
14.literary_structure: The passage builds in intensity: first the involuntary doing of what is hated (vv.14–17), then the failure of desire to produce action (vv.18–20), then the discovery of a competing 'law' waging war within (vv.21–23), finally the despairing cry and its thanksgiving answer (vv.24–25). The double acknowledgment of verse 25b—'I myself serve God's law with my mind but sin's law with my flesh'—is deliberately unresolved, setting up chapter 8's resolution through the Spirit. The rhetorical effect is a sustained cry for rescue that chapter 8 answers: 'There is therefore now no condemnation' (Romans 8:1).
14.theological_application: Romans 7:14–25 is the most psychologically penetrating account of moral failure in the New Testament. The conflict it describes is not between the desiring of evil and its reluctant pursuit, but between the genuine desire for good and the systematic inability to achieve it through self-directed effort. The 'wretched man' cry is not despair about specific sins but about the structural condition of existing in a body still subject to sin's gravitational pull. The passage destroys moralism not by permitting sin but by exposing the futility of self-help as the cure. The church must take seriously that the regenerate person is not exempt from this conflict—the indwelling Spirit produces the desire for righteousness that the flesh cannot fulfill unaided. The deliverance is christological: 'through Jesus Christ our Lord' (v.25). This means that Christian discipleship is not striving harder but dying more completely—learning to live from Christ's life rather than from one's own moral effort. The thanks-be-to-God answer anticipates chapter 8: the Spirit who dwells in believers is the power of the new age that overcomes what self-effort cannot.
14.christology: 'Who will deliver me from this body of death? Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord!' (Romans 7:24–25)—the answer to the existential cry is a person, not a program. The risen Jesus Christ is the one who has already done what the law demanded, conquered what sin produced (death), and now communicates his victory to those who are in him. The 'body of death' from which deliverance is sought is the mortal body still subject to sin's power; Christ's resurrection body is the firstfruits of the transformation that will ultimately deliver believers fully from that bondage (Romans 8:23; Philippians 3:21). The answer is anticipatory—chapter 7 ends with thanks before the full explanation of chapter 8—because the thanksgiving is grounded not in present experience but in the accomplished work of the one who has already conquered sin and death through his cross and resurrection.
